perturbation.original_text: (b) at any time during the period of the Purchase Agreement and for two years following the termination of the Purchase Agreement for any reason (the "Restrictive Period"), invest, carry on, engage, or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder (excluding ownership of not more than 3% of the outstanding shares of a publicly held Corporation if such ownership does not involve managerial or operational responsibility), manager, partner, joint venture, participant, or consultant, in any business enterprise (other than the Purchaser or any of its subsidiaries, affiliates, successors, or assigns) which (i) is located or operating, or soliciting customers located in the United States of America or any other country in which Purchaser sells products, and (ii) is or becomes, at any time during the Restrictive Period, engaged in the manufacture, assembly, sale, marketing, advertising, and distribution of golf balls or any golf related products.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) at any time during the period of the Purchase Agreement and for five years following the termination of the Purchase Agreement for any reason (the "Restrictive Period"), invest, carry on, engage, or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder (excluding ownership of not more than 3% of the outstanding shares of a publicly held Corporation if such ownership does not involve managerial or operational responsibility), manager, partner, joint venture, participant, or consultant, in any business enterprise (other than the Purchaser or any of its subsidiaries, affiliates, successors, or assigns) which (i) is located or operating, or soliciting customers located in the United States of America or any other country in which Purchaser sells products, and (ii) is or becomes, at any time during the Restrictive Period, engaged in the manufacture, assembly, sale, marketing, advertising, and distribution of golf balls or any golf related products.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to five years may be deemed unreasonably long under Utah law, potentially making it unenforceable. Non-compete agreements must be reasonable in time and geographic scope.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Utah Code addresses the enforceability of non-compete agreements. Utah law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable. A five-year restriction, depending on the specific facts and circumstances (like the employee's role and the nature of the business), might be deemed unreasonable, and thus unenforceable under Utah Code Ann. § 34-51-201.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-compete period from two to five years, which the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate could be deemed unreasonable under Utah law. The relevant law snippet, Utah Code Section 34-51-201, addresses the enforceability of non-compete agreements, and the Law Explanation states that a five-year restriction 'might be deemed unreasonable, and thus unenforceable'. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (the non-compete duration) violates state law, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Governing Law. This Agreement shall be construed under and governed ------------- by the laws of the State of Utah without regard to any conflict of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Governing Law. This Agreement shall be construed under and governed ------------- by the laws of the State of California without regard to any conflict of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California could conflict with Utah's strong public policy favoring freedom of contract, particularly if the agreement was negotiated and executed primarily in Utah and involves Utah-based parties. The choice of law provision might be unenforceable if it violates a fundamental policy of Utah law and Utah has a materially greater interest than California in the determination of the particular issue.
perturbation.law_explanation: This Restatement section details the rules for choice of law in contract cases. A California choice of law might be disregarded by a Utah court if it is contrary to a fundamental policy of Utah, which has a materially greater interest in the matter (like enforcing contracts fairly negotiated in Utah).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Utah to California introduces a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest that a Utah court might disregard the California choice of law if it violates a fundamental policy of Utah and Utah has a materially greater interest in the matter. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. The change could lead to a situation where California law is applied despite Utah's strong interest and policy, thus creating a contradiction under Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 In the event of a breach of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such breach may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such breach, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 In the event of a breach of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such breach may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such breach, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $500,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the liquidated damages to $500,000 could be considered an unenforceable penalty under Utah law if it's disproportionate to the actual harm reasonably anticipated from a breach. Utah courts scrutinize liquidated damages clauses to ensure they are a reasonable estimate of probable loss and not punitive.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 70A-2-718 governs liquidated damages in contracts. It states that damages for breach may be liquidated only at an amount that is reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual harm caused by the breach, the difficulties of proof of loss, and the inconvenience or nonfeasibility of otherwise obtaining an adequate remedy. A $500,000 amount may be considered unreasonable given the context of the agreement, making it an unenforceable penalty.
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the liquidated damages to $500,000. The Law Explanation states that Utah Code Ann. § 70A-2-718 governs liquidated damages, and the generated explanation says this amount may be considered unreasonable. The Perturbation Explanation states the increase could be seen as an unenforceable penalty if it's disproportionate to the actual harm reasonably anticipated. Because the change introduces a liquidated damages amount that potentially violates the legal requirement for reasonableness, a contradiction exists. The law explanation and the perturbation explanation supports the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.9 Dispute Resolution. Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the ------------------ parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be submitted for mediation in Salt Lake City, Utah. In the event mediation is unsuccessful, the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of an appropriate court within Salt Lake County, State of Utah, to hear and decide any controversy, claim, or dispute hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.9 Dispute Resolution. Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the ------------------ parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be resolved exclusively through binding arbitration conducted by a single arbitrator chosen by the Purchaser. The arbitration shall take place in a location of the Purchaser's choosing, and the arbitrator's decision shall be final and non-appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring mandatory, binding arbitration selected solely by the Purchaser, with no right to appeal, might be considered unconscionable and unenforceable, especially in Utah, if it is deemed one-sided and deprives the Undersigneds of a fair and neutral forum to resolve disputes. It lacks mutuality and fairness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Adams, 532 U.S. 105 (2001)
perturbation.law_citation: Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Adams, 532 U.S. 105 (2001)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this Supreme Court case generally upholds the enforceability of arbitration agreements under the FAA, it doesn't give a blank check to enforce any arbitration clause, no matter how unfair. An arbitration agreement imposed as a condition of employment (relevant here as the agreement is linked to the purchase agreement) where one party unilaterally controls the selection of the arbitrator and eliminates any avenue for appeal can be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under general contract law principles, which the FAA respects.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CIRCUIT CITY STORES, INC. v. ADAMS | Supreme Court | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you CIRCUIT CITY STORES, INC. v. ADAMS Supreme Court Syllabus CIRCUIT CITY STORES, INC. v. ADAMS 194 F. 3d 1070, reversed and remanded. Syllabus [Syllabus] [PDF] Opinion , Kennedy [Kennedy Opinion] [PDF] Dissent , Stevens [Stevens Dissent] [PDF] Dissent , Souter [Souter Dissent] [PDF] CIRCUIT CITY STORES, INC. v. ADAMS certiorari to the united states court of appeals for the ninth circuit No. 99–1379. Argued November 6, 2000—Decided March 21, 2001 A provision in respondent’s application for work at petitioner electronics retailer required all employment disputes to be settled by arbitration. After he was hired, respondent filed a state-law employment discrimination action against petitioner, which then sued in federal court to enjoin the state-court action and to compel arbitration pursuant to the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). The District Court entered the requested order. The Ninth Circuit reversed, interpreting §1 of the FAA—which excludes from that Act’s coverage “contracts of employment of seamen, railroad employees, or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce”—to exempt all employment contracts from the FAA’s reach. Held : The §1 exemption is confined to transportation workers. Pp. 3–16. (a) The FAA’s coverage provision, §2, compels judicial enforcement of arbitration agreements “in any … contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce.” In Allied-Bruce Terminix Cos. v. Dobson, 513 U. S. 265 , the Court interpreted §2’s “involving commerce” phrase as implementing Congress’ intent “to exercise [its] commerce power to the full.” Id., at 277. Pp. 3–5. (b) The Court rejects respondent’s contention that the word “transaction” in §2 extends only to commercial contracts, and that therefore an employment contract is not a “contract evidencing a transaction involving interstate commerce” at all. If that were true, the separate §1 exemption that is here at issue would be pointless. See, e.g., Pennsylvania Dept. of Public Welfare v. Davenport, 495 U. S. 552 , 562. Accordingly, any argument that arbitration agreements in employment contracts are not covered by the FAA must be premised on the language of the §1 exclusion itself. Pp. 5–6. (c) The statutory text forecloses the construction that §1 excludes all employment contracts from the FAA. Respondent relies on Allied-Bruce ’s expansive reading of “involving commerce” to contend that §1’s “engaged in … commerce” language should h
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces mediation with binding arbitration chosen solely by the Purchaser, eliminating any avenue for appeal. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be unconscionable and unenforceable, especially in Utah, and lacks mutuality and fairness. The Law Explanation supports this by saying that an arbitration agreement where one party unilaterally controls the selection of the arbitrator and eliminates any avenue for appeal can be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under general contract law principles, which the FAA respects. The Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Adams snippet mentions that all employment disputes had to be settled by arbitration. While the snippet focuses on the FAA's coverage, the Law Explanation clarifies that this case does not give a blank check to enforce any arbitration clause, no matter how unfair. Therefore, requiring mandatory arbitration selected solely by the Purchaser likely creates a legal contradiction as it could be deemed unconscionable.
